topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed, a grating soundtrack to my rising panic. The accusation hung in the air, thick and suffocating. I stared at the printed page, the highlighted sections mocking me with their clarity. Every sentence felt like a trap sprung, every borrowed idea a visible stain on my otherwise meticulous effort. My hands, usually steady when writing, were trembling slightly as I reread the professor's notes, searching for the precise moment where my work veered into the territory of theft. I felt a hot flush creep up my neck, a physical manifestation of the shame tightening around my chest. "It can't be true," I whispered, the sound barely audible above the shuffling of other students. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to mentally reconstruct the entire assignment, a futile exercise that only deepened the knot of dread in my stomach.

[story 2]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. The subject line was stark: 'Regarding Submission X.' My breath hitched. I spent the next hour pacing my small dorm room, the worn carpet seeming to absorb every anxious step. Each floorboard creak sounded like an indictment. I picked up my laptop, the screen glare harsh against my strained features. Looking at the similarity report, a cold wave washed over me, leaving a hollow, nauseating feeling. I started erasing lines of my draft, the eraser scraping against the paper sounding aggressively loud in the silence. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. *I messed up. I am fundamentally incapable of producing original thought.* The weight of expectation pressed down, heavy and inescapable.

[story 3]

When the committee members finally called me in, the room felt cavernous and intensely bright. I sat down, my posture rigid, trying to project an air of calm that felt utterly foreign. When the accusations began, my gaze dropped to the table surface, tracing the grain of the wood as if searching for an escape route. A dry, tight sensation formed in my throat. I offered brief, clipped responses, my voice sounding thin and reedy. Every word I spoke felt clumsy, inadequate, as if the very air was resisting my attempt to defend myself. I could feel the heat radiating from my cheeks, a burning shame that made me want to melt into the chair. I just wanted the scrutiny to stop, to let the suffocating pressure ease, but it only seemed to intensify, squeezing the air from my lungs with every unanswered question.
